column7: I'm not sure what your view is. Learning disabled people by definition need special attention because of the things they are not good at. A person in a wheelchair can get upstairs... with help (i.e., a ramp), so it's not a bad analogy. I don't see where people disagree with you. It just seems that some people want to use their disability as an excuse, and that sucks, but ANY handicapped person can do that. There are people in wheelchairs who sit around and feel sorry for themselves and people in wheelchairs who travel the world competing as athletes. It's the same thing.

@tattered-soccer, I see your point, but we have to be careful not to generalize. Not all learning disabled individuals use their disability as an excuse, and many face unique challenges that can't be overcome with just a ramp or special attention. We should focus on providing support and accommodations to help them thrive, rather than making assumptions about their abilities or motivations.

"@wide-chutney, well said. Let's keep the discussion focused on finding ways to support individuals with learning disabilities rather than making assumptions. @tattered-soccer, your point about not wanting to generalize is understood, but @wide-chutney's reminder to be cautious with our words is important. Let's continue the conversation with empathy and understanding."

column7:
>  But I don't think that people typically understand that there is a distinction at all between race and culture

On a macro level, these two tend to overlap quite heavily, and people tend to use over generalization all the time. Reminds me a bit of this topic (https://www.reddit.com/r/changemyview/comments/7r1ist/cmv_it_doesnt_make_sense_to_judge_an_individual/), but this time on sex/gender vs social class and not race vs culture. I think the same arguments could be used in both.

> also am very leery of accepting studies as evidence without being able to read what you are referencing

You can for example look at this one (2003, a bit old), checking the number of job interviews gotten from people with equal resume, only differing with "white sounding names" vs "african-american sounding names", realizing that the first got 1 interview every 10 contacts, for 1 every 15 for the second one

http://www.nber.org/papers/w9873.pdf

> But then again, all things being equal, I think any interviewer of any race will default to the applicant of his own race.

Pretty unsure about it, as a lot of countries have strong bias against certain races, and it may be internalized even by the discriminated ones. No sources to backup that, but I won't be surprised if a study showed that even an american black interviewer hire more easily white people than black ones.

You parse that out very well, but I don't think that people typically understand that there is a distinction at all between race and culture, especially since there can sometimes be no real difference in the cultures of two people who are racially in conflict (A white and black Southerner who both eat the same food and have nearly the same accent, for instance). 

I also am very leery of accepting studies as evidence without being able to read what you are referencing, but if the interviewer of these applicants is also white, chances are he might feel that he is culturally more in tune with the white applicant and will be able to communicate and relate to him better, not that he specifically does not like black people. But then again, *all things being equal*, I think any interviewer of any race will default to the applicant of his own race.
column19: ['Active', 'Not Applicable', 'Loves playing with technology and gadgets']

column7: @pureed-darby @deafening-slab you both make some good points, but I think we're forgetting one thing - personal experiences play a huge role in shaping our biases. Like, what if the interviewer had a bad experience with someone of a different race in the past? That could totally influence their decision, even if they don't realize it. And yeah, I get what you're saying about cultural differences, but sometimes it's hard to separate that from racial biases. Can we really say it's always one or the other?

"@timid-density, that's a great point about personal experiences influencing biases. @pureed-darby and @deafening-slab, your discussion on the complexity of distinguishing between cultural and racial biases is also valuable. Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on understanding different perspectives. Remember to avoid making assumptions about others' experiences or intentions."

column7:
Everything you said is about what men suffer. That says nothing about what women do or do not suffer.

For example;

82% of all juvenile sexual violence victims are female. 90% of adult rape victims are female

Or

Women are penalized for the same aggressive behaviors that men are rewarded for; and this translates directly into progression though management

Or tons of other stuff.

The problem with MRA stuff like what you wrote isn't that the problems aren't real.

The problem is that you insist on:

A) pretending other problems don't exist.

B) pretending there's no recent history of discrimination against women and no continued reverberations of that history

C) blaming women, and feminists specifically, for your problems.

D) acting like this is some zero sum competitive game.

A feminist, on the other hand, would not deny that the system we have harms men as well. For example, high male suicide rates are the result of our sexist expectations.

column7: That is not how economics works.

This exactly how economics works:
Money is initialy the representation of the trust from the market that he will effectively participate  *productively* in the society. But these value are dependant to a free market with little to no regulation distorting every measure in the economic. 

We care more about generating the value than really producing something usefull to  the society. We are competing against each other with brand of peanut butter when some can't afford the basic : water food and a place to live...

Forcing measure like this can pertube the economie because it is not to the people to decide but to the market and the market has no moral understanding only $$ (no blame just my fact of view)

Edit : lot of error in my text, I am not a native English speaker :(

@simple-pintail, I see what you're getting at, but I have to disagree. While it's true that the market can be distorted by excessive regulation, it's also important to consider the role of externalities and social welfare. Just because the market doesn't account for moral values doesn't mean we should abandon all regulation and let the pursuit of profit dictate everything. What about environmental degradation, worker exploitation, and social inequality? Don't those factors have a significant impact on the economy and society as a whole?
